speaker: interviewee
text: The paper, again?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: No? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Continuing now on our interview with survivor Jack Sandbrand. This is tape number five. Today's date is January 22, 1997, and we are in Rancho Bernardo, San Diego, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, you were telling me that you sent a letter back to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I what? Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sent a letter back to America to the uncles--

speaker: interviewee
text: --uncle of the three girls that--

speaker: interviewer
text: --whom you had saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you eventually got out of the Red Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just before the war was over, I was wounded on the outskirts-- outskirts of Berlin. A plane came along. I don't know if it was their plane-- it was, like, a week before the war was over-- or it was our plane. A bomb came down. I was picked up. I was standing on top of the truck. I was picked up and thrown against the wall, and the next thing I remember, I woke up in the hospital, Russian military hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I woke up, and the first thing I heard was a patient in another bed complaining that his toe hurts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I find out he had no legs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was black and blue, most of my body. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I could hardly move, but I knew I was alive when I woke up. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a doctor came in, and they examined me, and he touched every part of my body. And he says I don't have any broken bones or anything. [LAUGHS] How or what, I don't know. They had no x-rays in those days, not in the field, anyway. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I hear somebody say, the war is over. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And all I could say-- hallelujah, [LAUGHS] the war is over. I'm alive. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And I spent in the hospital about three weeks. I was coming to myself. I was able to get up. They fed us a little better. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a bath. They gave me a different uniform, clean shirt. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was up and around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sent back to my unit, which was what was called East Germany later on. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were part of the occupying forces of that area. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Considering the fact that my major told me, when we reached a certain point, advancing from Russia-- we advanced to Tarnopol. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew Tarnopol was very close to my hometown, and I was very anxious to know what happened to my family. I knew the town was published as liberated, and I asked him to let me go home for a day, and he said no. The first one after the war-- I went over, and I says, you remember what you said to me, the first one after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yes. He says, you can go home for two weeks. And they gave me a special release to go home for two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find when you got home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me three days to get home by train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I got home, most of the city was still in one piece. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came off the train in my uniform, and I walked to my home. And there was a Ukrainian family living in our house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The mezuzah was still on the door, though they didn't know what it was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I walked in, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the man of the home wasn't there. The woman was there with the children. And she-- she wanted to know what I wanted, and I told her that this-- my family lived in this house. I was born-- I don't know if I was born in that house-- but I was there. I grew up in that house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she said to me well, the house was empty. The Jews didn't come back. What happened to the Jews, she didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She says they were put on trains or whatever. And she says the city gave her the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went to see the commander in the city, and he was a Jew, of the police. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I spoke to him, and I told him about the house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he says it's an irony you came back. Not too many of us come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you want a house? Took me out-- we walked out to the middle of the city. He says, you see all these houses? They're all empty. He says, all the Jews that lived there are gone. He says, if you want to live here, you want a house, take-- move into any house you want. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was it. Did you ever find out what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did meet a woman who told me that my mother was in a group of women, about 200 of them, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that, either in 1942-- I'm not sure which date-- or the beginning of '43, were marched out into the woods by the Ukrainian collaborators, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] given shovels to dig, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told to undress, and they were all mowed down. There's a mass grave in the outskirts of the city. They have a light, there, burning, a eternal light. I think the Russians put it up. I'm not sure who. And she's one of them. My brother, my older brother, was put in a train and sent to one of the concentration camps. I'm not sure which one-- either Sobibor or Belzec, one of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He never came back. Grandma [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] must have died before, in the ghetto. She was in her 80s. Cousins and uncle, I have no idea what happened to them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back to your unit after visiting home?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided I didn't want to stay there anymore. I took off, got on the train, and went back to my unit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I come back, the unit is being disbanded. They had no use for mortar divisions anymore. [LAUGHS] And they ask me, what do I want to do? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I told them all that my family is all dead. I don't know what I'm going to do. And the same major says to me, he knows how I feel, my family is gone-- a Russian major. He says, you have no education. What are you going to do? Are you going to be a farmer? Are you going to be a factory worker? You worked in a factory making cylinders for airplanes. He says, what are you going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I can recommend you to go to a pilot school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is now being organized for Russian discharged, especially sergeants and all that. If you want to, I'll recommend you to be a pilot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I says fine, I'll be a pilot. It's in the city of Radom [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went, given papers, and I was transferred to this unit that the school was being trained to be pilots. The first--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. The first group that we joined, it was not flying. It was to teach us how the engine works. You know, everything that is pertained to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how should I say it-- book, [LAUGHS] you know, to be trained as a pilot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] While I was there, we were in regular barracks, we were able to go out into the city, and this is where I started meeting survivors-- Jewish survivors from that particular city who started coming home from Radom. And everyone I was meeting was one and only. They all lost their families-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mothers, fathers, wives, children, and all that stuff. And we started discussions. They had a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] group that started organizing to find out how many Jews survived, to help one or two who were coming back and they had nothing. You know, feeding them and all that stuff. I started getting together with these people, and I found out that most of them are in Stuttgart, Germany-- the survivors. Every once in awhile, one of them goes back to his hometown to find out what's going on, and he goes back to Stuttgart, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the American zone. And they tell me there is no future being in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is no future being in Russia. Many of them were collaborators. I started finding out the particulars of everything that happened during the war. These people were in camps, and they ran into a lot of these Ukrainian collaborators, the Russian collaborators, the Polish collaborators.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they told us what happened to them in the ghettos, you know what I'm saying? The Warsaw ghetto or the Radom ghetto or the Lodz ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I told them I don't want to be a pilot anymore. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they transferred me to a unit of military police, Russian military police, in Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there only a week. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It gave me enough time to see the city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I get to the train station in Lodz, and I meet these two guys, two Jewish guys. I'm still in touch with these two Jewish men. On the train, I was going from one-- back to Radom. We started discussing, and they tell me that they're running away to West Germany, and eventually to Israel, maybe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said to myself, [LAUGHS] what the hell am I going to do? With one, I'm in a unit, you know, and if I desert and they catch me, I'm in trouble. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What do I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to these people in Radom, these Jewish people, who were traveling back and forth-- survivors. He says it's a breeze to cross the border. They were going across the border. We got together, about a half a dozen men and about four women. I'm still in my uniform. The two guys are in their Polish uniforms, and we all got on the train, and we went as close as we could to East Germany, to the German border. We spent the night. We got up early in the morning, and we crossed the border to the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- one of the Jewish men who travelled back and forth knew exactly where to go. I have a feeling that he was somehow connected with the Jewish underground. At the time, they were trying to get as many men they could from the Russian-- Jewish men-- from the Russian or the Polish armies to cross the border to go and eventually go to Palestine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were stopped by one Russian guard. He gave him a bribe. He took away a watch from one women, and we crossed the border. And the following bor-- day, we were in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hitchhiked. A truck stopped, we hitchhiked-- an American truck or somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole group or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole group together. And we wound up in the Munich Museum for the night. They made, in the Munich Museum at that time, a stopover to sleep over. Mind you, this was '45, right after the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we slept over there. And the next day, the group broke up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we traveled from camp to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we stopped off in Stuttgart, and then we went to Bergen-Belsen, where we came in, in our uniforms-- this is-- [LAUGHS] I just remembered it. The two guy-- the two guys in the Polish uniforms and myself, we come into Bergen Belsen. And this Polish guy brings us in, and he says, you can sleep in this room. We were tired, we slept, and around 5:00 in the morning, the door gets kicked in. A half a dozen British soldiers with sub-machine guns come into the room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They pointed at us. We get up. Who are you? They thought we were with the-- with the underground, with the Israeli underground. So we explained to them that we were survivors, and we lost our family. And we were looking for, you know-- they had a translator, a Polish translator.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the Polish guy went to the British to tell them about us. And after we saw what was going on there, we also saw part of the Jewish Brigade was there. After a day or two we decided we didn't want to stay in the British zone, and we went back to Stuttgart in the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get papers? How did you get recognition?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Stuttgart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The UNRRA was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: UNRRA is? What is UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: The United Nations Relief Organization. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the HIAS was already connected with the UNRRA to help-- you know what the HIAS is-- to help Jewish survivors. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I registered my name to see also-- and I was looking at their listings-- if anybody in my family maybe was registered, or-- nobody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They told-- they asked me could I drive a vehicle? I said yes, I could drive a Studebaker truck. They gave me the Studebaker truck, and I was bringing in supplies to the Jewish camp, which was in the city of Stuttgart, part of the city. Most camps were usually on the outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- what the French did, actually, when they liberated the city of Stuttgart, they took the Jews out of the camp, they evacuated all the Germans from a two or three block area, and they gave these apartment houses to the Jews. Those were the good French. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was part of that group of displaced persons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started working with this Jewish woman who was part of the United Nations. Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a coincidence, her name was the same as the doctor's name-- Mrs. Levine, and she was from New York City. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She spoke Yiddish, also. I didn't speak any English at all. And we-- as we were talking, you know, and all that stuff, she says to me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jack, you have any relatives anywhere? I told her, yes, my mother has three sisters in America, and my father had four brothers in America-- Zeichners. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She says well, let's see what we can do for you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then I remembered I had the telegram from the man in Tel Aviv. I had the letter from Mr. Weiss in Fort Hamilton Parkway. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said to her, you know, I remember one name of my aunt, a Mrs. Bretholtz that lived in the Bronx. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She took all that down, and I says, Mr. Weiss-- Mr. and Mrs. Weiss-- he's also my uncle. I lied a little. [LAUGHS] She says, no problem. And also I remembered, my grandfather, before he died, he used to be an agent for an American company, selling tickets for people to go to America. And before the war, he bought four tickets, before he died, for our family to go to the States. And the war started, and that was the end of it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She started looking. She took me down to the American Consul. I have the original paper they gave me from the American Consul, who I am. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I have the original ticket for the ship [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that brought me here. It was the second Navy ship, [? Marite ?] Perch, that brought me to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sail out of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bremenhofen. It was May 9th-- May 10th, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she arranged for you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She went, she applied-- she said I had relatives in the States. She applied for the visa. The HIAS gave me-- got the visa for me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They found the tickets that were still outstanding, that my grandfather bought before the war. I was given one of them, and the other three I told them, give them away to whoever. And on May 24th, we arrived in New York Harbor. I didn't get off until May 26th, which was my birthday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's practically the end of the story. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's actually the beginning of a story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A beginning. A new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so here you are in America, and you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Here you are In America, and you were what, 21 at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 Years of age, and I was alone. Most of the passengers who came off the boat had somebody. I knew I had somebody, but I wasn't sure where. I had the address of Mr. and Mrs. Weiss. That was it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There were a few of us that were all single men or single women. The HIAS came over to us, and they asked us, do we have anybody helping us, picking us up? I said, no, not that I know of. And they brought us down to 103rd Street and Broadway to the Hotel Marseilles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that night they made a party for us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some elderly Jewish ladies came, with all makeup, [LAUGHS] in their hats and their high heels, and all [YIDDISH], all dressed up. And one of them shoves a cake-- a chocolate cake-- in my hands. [LAUGHS] It was funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what can we do for you? You know, they were anxious. We were one of the first refugees who survived the war and that were coming into the country. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the next day, I came down to the lobby, and I had the address of Mr. and Mrs. Weiss, and I asked them-- I asked them, maybe they have a phone.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy gets the telephone book and looks it up. And sure enough, they have a phone. They live in Fort Hamilton Parkway, Brooklyn. He gets the phone. He tells me how to dial, you know. And it's Friday, late in the afternoon, and Mrs. Weiss answers the phone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she-- and I says to her, [YIDDISH] Yiddish? Do you speak Yiddish? And she says, yes. And I asked her-- I tell her, you know who I am? And she says, no. [LAUGHS] And I says I'm the man who helped your nieces during the war after the-- after liberation. And her first words are [YIDDISH]? You are here already? Where are you? Please come to my house, now. We found your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: What they did is they put it in the Jewish paper, There may be still a record of it. [LAUGHS] And they advertised, and a woman who was a friend of my mother's in the old country read about it. She knew me, she claimed, before the war. She came to the States just before the war started, and she went running to my aunt's hou-- store. My aunt had a dress store in the Bronx, [? Pelham ?] Parkway. And she says to her, Fanny, Fanny, your sister's son is alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jankel is alive. Jack is alive. And here is an address. People are-- advertised they heard from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause right here, Jack, to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Sure.
